metadata.title: Build a MCP Payload Aggregator: Raw Dump vs. Aggregated Response

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Agentic CLI — build it, measure it, take it apart.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Nik Bear Brown. Agentic CLI — build it, measure it, take it apart.

beats.narration_text: A database query that returns eighteen hundred forty-two objects when you need six numbers is not a data retrieval problem — it's a tool design problem that gets worse every time you call it. The tool does no computation. The agent does all of it. And eighteen hundred rows in context is eighteen hundred rows the model has to reason through before getting to the answer.

shot.notes: diagram: database returns 1842-row CSV, all rows flow into context window; agent icon surrounded by data; caption: '1842 rows when you need 6 numbers = tool design problem'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A database query that returns eighteen hundred forty-two objects when you need six numbers is not a data retrieval problem — it's a tool design problem that get

beats.narration_text: Ask for the aggregator. In the terminal: claude — summarize monthly spend from this data. Return only: query description, date range, record count, monthly totals, source table name. Do not include individual records. Format as JSON.

props.command:
claude "Summarize monthly spend from this data.
  Return only: query description, date range,
  record count, monthly totals (month + total),
  source table name.
  No individual records. Format as JSON." < aggregated_payload.json

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Ask for the aggregator. In the terminal: claude — summarize monthly spend from this data. Return only: query description, date range, record count, monthly tota

beats.narration_text: Read the aggregator. It takes a raw CSV dump of transaction records, computes monthly totals in pandas — sum, group by month — and packages the result as a provenance-enriched JSON payload: query description, date range, record count, monthly totals array, source table name. The raw rows never enter the context window.

props.code:
# payload_aggregator.py — Claude Code output
import pandas as pd, json, sys

def aggregate(csv_path, table_name):
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_path, parse_dates=['date'])
    monthly = (df.groupby(df['date'].dt.to_period('M'))['amount']
                 .sum().reset_index())
    monthly.columns = ['month', 'total']
    return {
        'query': 'monthly spend summary',
        'date_range': [str(df['date'].min()), str(df['date'].max())],
        'record_count': len(df),
        'monthly_totals': monthly.to_dict('records'),
        'source_table': table_name
    }

result = aggregate(sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2])
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2, default=str))

beats.narration_text: The token comparison chart appears. Raw dump bar fills to fourteen thousand tokens — the full CSV. Aggregated payload bar fills to one hundred forty tokens. Both bars then show the same analytical output alongside them: March was the peak month. The finding is identical. The cost is one percent.

shot.notes: horizontal bar chart: RAW DUMP bar fills to 14000 tokens (crimson); AGGREGATED bar fills to 140 tokens (green); both bars show same output text: 'peak: March — $42,310'; annotation: '1% of the cost, same finding'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The token comparison chart appears. Raw dump bar fills to fourteen thousand tokens — the full CSV. Aggregated payload bar fills to one hundred forty tokens. Bot

beats.narration_text: Now add a verify step. In the terminal: claude — update the aggregator to add a verify-one-month endpoint that queries March only and returns just the March total and row count. Show that provenance makes re-verification cheap.

props.command:
claude "update payload_aggregator.py:
  -> add verify_month(month) endpoint
  -> queries only that month
  -> returns total + row count
  -> show re-verification is cheap"

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Now add a verify step. In the terminal: claude — update the aggregator to add a verify-one-month endpoint that queries March only and returns just the March tot

beats.narration_text: The verify-March call returns: total forty-two thousand three hundred ten, row count one hundred twelve, source table transactions underscore 2026. Thirty tokens. The provenance metadata in the aggregated payload told us exactly how to re-verify without reloading all eighteen hundred rows.

shot.notes: verify endpoint output: JSON with month: March-2026 / total: 42310 / row_count: 112 / source: transactions_2026; annotation: '30 tokens to re-verify'; vs '14000 tokens to reload raw data'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The verify-March call returns: total forty-two thousand three hundred ten, row count one hundred twelve, source table transactions underscore 2026. Thirty token

beats.narration_text: Returning everything is not more honest — it is more expensive and less useful. Returning the computed result plus provenance is the design principle: the tool does the aggregation, the agent does the reasoning. Provenance makes the result verifiable without a full reload. That is the contract.

shot.notes: teardown card: 'tool does aggregation / agent does reasoning'; two bullets: 'raw dump: honest but expensive and re-read every time' / 'aggregated + provenance: verifiable without reload'; 'that is the contract'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Returning everything is not more honest — it is more expensive and less useful. Returning the computed result plus provenance is the design principle: the tool

beats.narration_text: Your move: find one MCP tool that returns a full table when a summary would do. Add the aggregation in the tool layer. Next reel: the diff-first change auditor — because the same principle applies to code changes.

shot.notes: next steps: '1) identify raw-dump tool  2) add aggregation in tool layer  3) include provenance fields  4) add spot-verify endpoint'; teaser: 'next: diff-first change auditor'
